blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_OVERLINE
blocks.value: In Memoriam

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_TITLE
blocks.value: Manashi Chakrabarti Foundation

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_SUBTITLE
blocks.hint: Subtitle paragraph under the section title.
blocks.value: A tribute to the life, spirit, and enduring legacy of Manashi Chakrabarti — whose memory inspires our mission of research and care for children with life-threatening blood disorders.

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_BIO_PARA_1
blocks.value: Born as the first child to Ganendranath Chakrabarti and Uma Devi in 1939, Manashi was very special to her parents. Her father, an eminent pathologist, noted some rare talents in the young girl – a mind that could perceive the abstract and express it through her paintings and sketches. As is the story of many such families of her time, Manashi's talents were suffocated by the existence of a middle class family that found painting to be an unnecessary pastime.

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_BIO_PARA_2
blocks.value: At the age of 20, Manashi got married to Bimalangshu. Bimalangshu hailed from a family in Rajshahi, a part of East Bengal that became East Pakistan in 1947. Bimalangshu, a bright medical student at that time, joined the army and became a distinguished soldier and anaesthetist. Manashi adapted to living in a large joint family, but, the promise and talents were submerged in her chores. She raised two children, Mousumi and Suparno. In 1974, she lost her younger brother at the age of 21. This changed her life forever. She started to seek the truth about this world and the hereafter.

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_BIO_PARA_3
blocks.value: Ganendranath had retired as a frustrated academic pathologist, getting little recognition for his brilliance. This pained Manashi. She felt that her children must fulfil the academic promise that she and her father were not allowed to express. Suparno went abroad to earn further expertise in medicine, promising to come back to her side and fulfil the family's mission of research in medicine.

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_BIO_PARA_4
blocks.value: On 13th June 2005, Bimalangshu was admitted to the hospital intensive care with a chest infection. Manashi was by his side for the next few days. On 17th June, she suffered a massive heart attack, which she had smilingly defeated when only a few days old, to grace this world with all her talents.

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_BIO_PARA_5
blocks.value: Death could not have taken Manashi's legacy away from us. It will live on with this charity founded by her children to fulfil the family's mission of research in medicine and the care of children with life-threatening blood disorders.

blocks.label: ABOUT_MCF_POEM
blocks.hint: Poem shown in a highlighted side block.
blocks.value:
'It's time to say good-bye
But, please don't cry.
When I have left for a different shore,
Just gently shut the door.
Yet let the memories stay,
Bright as the sunshine may.
Then, every drop of rain you'll find,
Leaves a rainbow behind.'

subsections.title: Research Card 1 — Children with Blood Disorders

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_OVERLINE
blocks.value: 1. Children with Blood Disorders

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_TITLE
blocks.value: Supporting the cause of children suffering from blood disorders

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_PARA_1
blocks.value: Blood cancers account for half of childhood malignancies. They are broad of two types, myeloid and lymphoid. Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia or ALL account for 90% of blood cancers between the ages of 1-16 years. Diligent research and exhaustive clinical trials have now made it one of the most curable cancers. In the western world, 80% of children diagnosed with ALL get cured with chemotherapy alone. Of the other 20% which relapse, 50-60% are cured with further treatment including a bone marrow transplantation.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_PARA_2
blocks.value: In developing countries including India, the majority of children with ALL fail to access proper healthcare facilities and even if they do so, a large number of the default treatment due to logistic and financial constraints.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_PARA_3
blocks.value: Our endeavor is to provide awareness, access, and support to all such children who could lead a happy and healthy life and productively contribute to the development of the society. Thus, the journey does not end with successful treatment. That is the beginning of a long and productive life through proper guidance and support for educational and psychological rehabilitation.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_PARA_4
blocks.value: The success in treatment of cancer depends on the understanding and amalgamation of individual biology and the environment we live in. The factors predisposing to childhood cancers are poorly understood. In a country where air and water pollution is on the rise and many carcinogenic substances are used in daily life without regulation, we need to know the cause of blood cancers in our children.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH1_PARA_5
blocks.value: Disease biology differs from geographical and ethnic variations. Little do we know if the biology of childhood leukemia in India is different from that in the west. Uncompromised research on each of these areas is the need of the hour. Our organization is striving to gather the infrastructure and human resources to start answering these questions. We invite all interested researchers, collaborators, philanthropists to join us in this endeavor.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH2_OVERLINE
blocks.value: 2. BMT Research

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH2_TITLE
blocks.value: Research on Bone Marrow Transplantation (BMT) in Children

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH2_PARA_1
blocks.value: BMT from a donor or Allogeneic BMT is often the only curative treatment for advanced blood cancers. If a matched donor is not available in the family, the patient can go for a transplant from an unrelated donor.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH2_PARA_2
blocks.value: We inherit half of our HLA genes from each parent and pass it on likewise to our children. Thus, HLA or tissue type is 50% matched between the children and their parents. This is called a Haploidentical match.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH2_PARA_3
blocks.value: We were the first in the world to point out that this approach for children was wrought with high risks of rejection and Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD). Through years of diligent clinical research, we have now developed the most effective way of transplanting such children from a parent or a haploidentical donor. This discovery has changed the lives of many such children. Our organization has provided expertise and logistic support for such research activities over the last decade.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH3_OVERLINE
blocks.value: 3. Cancer Survivorship

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH3_TITLE
blocks.value: Surviving after Blood Cancer

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH3_PARA_1
blocks.value: Following the ordeal of going through treatment for blood cancer, the biggest challenge lies in rehabilitation and long-term surveillance. Growth and mental development of the survivors of childhood cancer is of paramount importance.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH3_PARA_2
blocks.value: In addition, cancer drugs can also have an effect on the heart and the lungs and need regular monitoring. Society at large should take the responsibility of rehabilitating the survivors of childhood cancer, so that they can take up a lead role in society in future.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH4_OVERLINE
blocks.value: 4. Thalassemia

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH4_TITLE
blocks.value: A life free of Thalassemia

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH4_PARA_1
blocks.value: β-Thalassemia Major is the commonest genetic disorder in India. About 10,000 children with thalassemia are born every year. Despite the improvement in supportive care, the long-term outcome of children with transfusion-dependent β thalassemia in developing countries is disappointing.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH4_PARA_2
blocks.value: Recent data from WHO confirms that about 12% of children born with transfusion-dependent β thalassemia are actually transfused, and less than 5% receive adequate iron chelation. BMT from a matched sibling donor was established as a curative treatment for this condition in the early eighties. However, only 10-20% of thalassemia sufferers find a matched family donor.

blocks.label: ABOUT_RESEARCH4_PARA_3
blocks.value: Our endeavor is to develop facilities for transfusion and chelation for children under the optimum guidance of pediatric hematologists. We also provide expertise in curative treatment for this condition.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is Thalassemia and Types of Thalassemia?

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder characterised by low haemoglobin (Haemoglobin is an iron rich protein present within the Red Blood Cells of the body. Haemoglobin is responsible for carrying oxygen to all parts of the body). Normal haemoglobin binds iron with alfa and beta globin (protein) chains. Due to genetic mutations in both the genes inherited from the parents, either alfa or beta chains are absent or reduced. This results in precipitation of other globin chains inside the red blood cells, leading to deformity of the red cells. These deformed red cells get destroyed either within the bone marrow (spongy material within the bones) or spleen resulting in anemia (Pallor). This is called Thalassemia Major.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: When an individual inherits only one abnormal gene, the manifestations are very mild. This is called Thalassemia Minor or Thalassemia Trait.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: The commonest form of Thalassemia in India is Beta-Thalassemia, where Beta-globin chains are deficient. When instead of absolute absence of one type of globin chain, there is reduction only; the condition is called Thalassemia Intermedia.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ1_A4
blocks.value: Other Thalassemia like Syndromes such as HbE, HbD etc. when co-inherited with Beta-thalassemia gene give rise to Thalassemia Intermedia.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Symptoms

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: Symptoms of Thalassemia Major

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: The symptoms appear between 3-7 years age. The child starts becoming listless, irritable and pale. The parents often visit the doctor several times before this is detected. The spleen enlarges in size and the eyes turn yellow from jaundice. The child is unable to grow and play like other children of his age.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_H2
blocks.value: Symptoms of Thalassemia Intermedia

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_A2
blocks.value: This depends on the severity of the affection of globin gene production. In more severe form, the symptoms are same as Thalassemia major, but they start later in life. Often they don't need regular blood transfusion.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_H3
blocks.value: Symptoms of Thalassemia Trait

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ2_A3
blocks.value: This does not produce any symptom and is often detected on routine blood tests. The red cell morphology is abnormal but the Hb is usually near normal.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_H1
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: An astute haematologist easily picks up the diagnosis on routine blood tests.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_H2
blocks.value: Hemoglobin Electrophoresis

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: This is needed to confirm the diagnosis. HbF is the major component with very little of normal haemoglobin (HbA).

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_H3
blocks.value: Genetic Tests

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_A3
blocks.value: Genetic tests are done to test for the mutations of Thalassemia. This is needed in the following situations:

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ3_LIST
blocks.hint: Bullets under the Genetic Tests heading.
value: When the child is already transfused and the diagnosis cannot be confirmed by electrophoresis.
When a couple wants to confirm the status of the foetus i.e. antenatal testing.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Treatment of Thalassemia Major

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_INTRO
blocks.value: There are two approaches to the treatment of Thalassemia major:

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: 1. Transfusion and Chelation (Removal of iron from body)

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_A1a
blocks.value: This involves regular red cell transfusion to maintain the Hb above 12 gm% and not let it drop below 9.5 gm%. This enables normal growth and development.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_A1b
blocks.value: However, such regular transfusion loads our body with excess iron which affects the heart, liver and other organs. There are medicines, both Intravenous and Oral, to remove excess iron from our body. Effective iron chelation helps in maintaining organ function in regularly transfused patients.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_SE_INTRO
blocks.value: Other side effects of transfusion include:

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_SE_LIST
value: Development of antibodies against red cells.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_A1c
blocks.value: During chelation, the effect on eyes and ears has to be monitored. Odd infections with bacteria like Yersinia can also occur.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_H2
blocks.value: 2. Bone Marrow Transplantation (BMT)

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_A2a
blocks.value: ALLOGENEIC Bone Marrow Transplantation (BMT) is the only curative treatment for this condition. This works by replacement of diseased stem cells with healthy donor stem cells. Once healthy stem cells start producing normal red cells with normal Hb, the patient is cured.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ4_A2b
blocks.value: This doesn't change the genetic composition of the patient, it only changes the genetic composition of the bone marrow.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_Q
blocks.value: Role of BMT — Best Age, Donor, Outcomes

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_H1
blocks.value: What is the best age for patients with Thalassemia to undergo Bone Marrow Transplantation?

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_A1
blocks.value: Best age is between 2 to 5 years. However, successful BMT has been carried out in older children and young adults.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_H2
blocks.value: Who can be the donor?

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_DONOR_LIST
value: Matched Family donor is the best option.
A person who has Thalassemia trait can be a donor for a patient with Thalassemia major.
Results with Matched Unrelated Donor are improving.
Recent studies have shown that HAPLOIDENTICAL (Half Matched) FAMILY DONOR might be a viable option for patients with Thalassemia major.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_H3
blocks.value: How do we predict the success or failure of BMT in a particular patient?

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_A3
blocks.value: Prof. Lucarelli from Pessaro, Italy had proposed a scoring system for patients undergoing BMT for Thalassemia. This takes into account the effectiveness of transfusion, chelation and degree of iron overload. The patients are classified as Class I, Class II, Class III.

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_H4
blocks.value: Outcome based on Pessaro Classification: Thalassemia free Survival

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_H5
blocks.value: Do patients with Thalassemia Intermedia need BMT?

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_A5
blocks.value: Following categories of patients need Bone Marrow Transplantation:

blocks.label: THAL_PED_FAQ5_INTERMEDIA_LIST
value: Those who choose to be cured knowing the risks and benefits

subsections.title: FAQ — What Is Sickle Cell Anemia?

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What Is Sickle Cell Anemia?

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Sickle Cell Anaemia is the most common form of sickle cell disease (SCD). SCD is a serious disorder in which the body makes sickle-shaped red blood cells. "Sickle-shaped" means that the red blood cells are shaped like a crescent.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: Normal red blood cells are disc-shaped and look like doughnuts without holes in the centre. They move easily through the blood vessels. Red blood cells contain an iron-rich protein called haemoglobin. This protein carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: Sickle cells contain abnormal haemoglobin called sickle hemoglobin or hemoglobin S. Sickle hemoglobin causes the cells to develop a sickle, or crescent shape.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ1_A4
blocks.value: Sickle cells are stiff and sticky. They tend to block blood flow in the blood vessels of the limbs and organs. Blocked blood flow can cause pain and organ damage. It can also raise the risk for infection.

subsections.title: FAQ — Causes & Who Is at Risk?

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Causes & Who Is at Risk?

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: When both parents have a normal gene and an abnormal gene, each child has a 25 percent chance of inheriting two normal genes; a 50 percent chance of inheriting one normal gene and one abnormal gene; and a 25 percent chance of inheriting two abnormal genes.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ2_A2
blocks.value: People who inherit a sickle hemoglobin gene from one parent and a normal gene from the other parent have sickle cell trait. Their bodies make both sickle hemoglobin and normal hemoglobin. People who have sickle cell trait usually have few, if any, symptoms and lead normal lives. However, they can pass the sickle hemoglobin gene to their children.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ2_A3
blocks.value: People who inherit both sickle hemoglobin genes from both parents develop Sickle Cell Disease. Their bodies make mostly sickle hemoglobin.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ2_A4
blocks.value: Sickle cell anemia is most common in people whose families come from Africa, South or Central America (especially Panama), Caribbean islands, Mediterranean countries (such as Turkey, Greece, and Italy), India, and Saudi Arabia.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Signs & Symptoms

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: The signs and symptoms of sickle cell anemia vary. Some people have mild symptoms. Others have very severe symptoms and are often hospitalized for treatment. Sickle cell anemia is present at birth, but many infants don't show any signs until after 4 months of age.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: The most common signs and symptoms are linked to anemia and pain. Symptoms may include:

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ3_LIST
value: Severe pain in arms, back, abdomen and legs
Strokes — one-sided weakness or paralysis in arms, face or legs; or finding difficulty in talking, walking or seeing
Shortness of breath or difficulty in breathing
Blood flow blockage in liver or spleen
Hand & foot syndrome i.e. swelling in the feet and hands

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count and PERIPHERAL Blood Smear

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_A1
blocks.value: The characteristic finding of SCD is circulating sickle shaped red cells. Routine examination of the blood count reveals anemia. Reticulocytosis i.e. increase in young red blood cells is almost always seen.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_H2
blocks.value: Hemoglobin Electrophoresis

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_A2
blocks.value: This is the cornerstone of diagnosis and is used to detect Hemoglobin S. HbA2 and often HbF are increased in SCD.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_H3
blocks.value: Prenatal Diagnosis

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ4_A3
blocks.value: May also be detected by examining the DNA from the chorionic villus biopsy or from the cells of amniotic fluid.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ5_Q
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ5_H1
blocks.value: Supportive Treatment

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ5_LIST1
value: All the infections should be identified and should be treated early.
Treatment of painful crisis or other complications.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ5_H2
blocks.value: Specific Treatment

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ5_LIST2
blocks.hint: Specific treatment bullets (bold term at start of each line).
value: Hydroxyurea: Data have shown that it decreases the painful crisis episodes. This drug decreases Neutrophil concentration and stickiness of red cells and increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF).
Red cell Transfusion: Used frequently in Sickle Cell Disease to increase Hb concentration and decrease the concentration of sickle red cells.
Exchange red cell transfusions: This is done to reduce the concentration of sickle cells without increasing the concentration of red cells too much.

subsections.title: FAQ — Role of BMT in Sickle Cell Disease

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_Q
blocks.value: Role of BMT in Sickle Cell Disease

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A1
blocks.value: ALLOGENEIC BMT was first carried out in 1980 in a patient with Acute Leukemia who also had Sickle Cell Disease. Since then several centers in the Europe and North America have successfully performed BMT for Sickle Cell Disease.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A2
blocks.value: This shows that 90% of the most severely affected patients with Sickle Cell Disease are cured with BMT if they have a matched family donor.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A3
blocks.value: However, very few patients with Sickle Cell Disease have a Matched Family Donor. The Unrelated Donor Transplants are often complicated by severe Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD).

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A4
blocks.value: BMT in Sickle Cell Disease has produced the best results one can expect in any severe inherited disease.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A5
blocks.value: Recent studies have shown that Haploidentical BMT can cure the majority of patients with Sickle Cell Disease with nearly 100% chance of finding a donor.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_H1
blocks.value: How is Conditioning for BMT done in Sickle Cell Disease?

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A6
blocks.value: Primarily Reduced Intensity conditioning is used in adults and conventional high dose chemotherapy or radiation is the preferred option in children.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_H2
blocks.value: Who can be a donor for BMT?

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_A7
blocks.value: Although we prefer a matched family donor, a Half matched (Haploidentical) family donor or an unrelated donor provide excellent survival.

blocks.label: SC_PED_FAQ6_HIGHLIGHT
blocks.value: WE OFFER HAPLOIDENTICAL BMT FOR PATIENTS WITH SICKLE CELL DISEASE LACKING A MATCHED FAMILY DONOR.

blocks.label: SC_PED_OVERLINE
blocks.value: Pediatric BMT — Dr. Mahak Agarwal

blocks.value: Primarily Reduced Intensity conditioning is used in adults and conventional high dose chemotherapy is used in children.

blocks.label: SC_AD_FAQ4_H5
blocks.value: Who can be a donor for BMT?

blocks.label: SC_AD_FAQ4_A6
blocks.value: Although we prefer a matched family donor, a Half matched (Haploidentical) family donor or an unrelated donor can also be used.

blocks.label: SC_AD_FAQ4_HIGHLIGHT
blocks.value: WE OFFER HAPLOIDENTICAL BMT FOR PATIENTS WITH SICKLE CELL DISEASE LACKING A MATCHED FAMILY DONOR.

blocks.label: SC_AD_OVERLINE

blocks.label: SC_AD_TITLE
blocks.value: Sickle Cell Anemia in Adults

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is Aplastic Anemia?

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: The blood cells have a limited lifespan. Red blood cells survive 3 months, white cells survive for 24 hours and platelets survive for 5 days. The bone marrow is an amazing organ which produces billions of blood cells every day to keep up with the requirements of our body. When the bone marrow fails, the cell production slows down and often stops. This is called aplastic anemia.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: The incidence of Aplastic anemia in Asian countries is 5 times higher than in Europe or USA.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Causes

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ2_INTRO
blocks.value: Aplastic anemia results from damage to the mother cells or stem cells which are no longer able to produce normal blood cells. This can happen due to the following causes:

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ2_LIST
value: Chemicals and toxins such as benzene and pesticides
Drugs such as arsenic, gold, painkillers and some antibiotics
Inherited conditions such as Fanconi's Anemia
Most commonly, the cause is not known and thought to be due to an immune mediated attack by the body on its own stem cells.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis & Classification

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: The diagnosis of aplastic anemia needs awareness of the condition and a strong index of suspicion. The patient can present with fever due to infection, bleeding and bruising due to low platelet count and fatigue due to anemia.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: A routine blood test shall show at least two of the three cell lines of red cells, white cells and platelets being low. A marrow biopsy is mandatory to confirm the diagnosis and rule out other causes of marrow failure like leukemia.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_A3
blocks.value: It is important to look for a causative factor and stop any drug or exposure to chemical that might have caused it.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_A4
blocks.value: The investigations include blood tests to look for infections, autoimmune diseases like lupus and chromosomal breakage study to rule out Fanconi's anemia.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_H1
blocks.value: Classification

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ3_LIST
value: Severe: neutrophil count less than 500 cells/microliter (normal 1500-6000/microliter)
Very Severe: neutrophil count less than 200 cells/microliter

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Treatment & Role of BMT

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: Supportive Therapy

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_A1
blocks.value: We cannot survive without functioning blood cells. Immediate treatment involves transfusion of red cells and platelets and treatment of infections. However, the patient must be referred to a center where curative treatment can be given as soon as possible. Repeated blood transfusions reduce the success rate of BMT.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_H2
blocks.value: Immunosuppressive Treatment

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_A2
blocks.value: Antithymocyte globulin, a drug produced from horse or rabbit by injecting human lymphocytes in them, is the main drug. It suppresses the immune system and allows the marrow to recover. This drug is given with another oral medicine called Cyclosporine. About 60-70% of the patients improve with this treatment. However, it takes 3-6 months for the marrow to recover and many patients may relapse or develop other marrow disorders in the future.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_H3
blocks.value: Blood and Marrow Transplantation (BMT)

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_A3
blocks.value: BMT cures 90% of the patients with aplastic anemia if carried out early and from a matched family donor. BMT is the treatment of choice for young patients with a matched sibling donor.

blocks.label: AA_PED_FAQ4_A4
blocks.value: For patients without a matched family donor, HAPLOIDENTICAL DONOR TRANSPLANTATION is now giving results as good as a matched family donor. At BLOODS-R-US, we offer the best survival for patients with aplastic anemia through BMT.

blocks.label: AA_PED_OVERLINE
blocks.value: Pediatric BMT — Dr. Suparno Chakrabarti

The bone marrow is an amazing organ which produces billions of blood cells every day to keep up with the requirements of our body. subsections.title: FAQ — What is ALL in Children?

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is ALL in Children?

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Acute Lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), is a cancer that starts from white blood cells called lymphocytes in the bone marrow (the soft inner part of the bones, where new blood cells are generated).

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: The term "acute" means that the leukemia can progress quickly, and if not treated, would probably be fatal within a few months. 'Lymphoblastic' means it develops from early (immature) forms of lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia is the commonest childhood cancer. This arises from abnormalities in the precursors of normal lymphocytes which are otherwise a normal part of our immune system necessary to fight infections. These abnormal cells rapidly fill up the bone marrow space suppressing the normal cells.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ1_A4
blocks.value: In most cases, the disease is characterised by certain abnormalities in the chromosomes which promote abnormal growth of certain lymphoid cells rather than maturation to normal lymphocytes.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Causes & Symptoms

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: This is not known in most cases. However, exposure to radiation, exposure to high voltage electric wires and chemical exposures are few known risk factors.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: Symptoms

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ2_LIST
value: Bruising easily or with no obvious cause
Bleeding from the gums or nose
A fine rash of dark red spots (called purpura)
Pain in the bones or joints

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis & Classification

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_H1
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: The characteristic finding of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia is high white blood cell count with low haemoglobin and platelets. Routine examination of the blood under the microscope may confirm the presence of abnormal cells (blasts).

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_H2
blocks.value: Bone Marrow Examination

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: This is necessary for confirmation of the diagnosis. There must be more than 30% of abnormal cells (blasts) in the marrow for it to be called Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_H3
blocks.value: Flow Cytometry

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A3
blocks.value: ALL has two broad subtypes- B cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia and T cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia. There are further subtypes in the individual categories. This has got implications on the choice of treatment and the likely outcome.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_H4
blocks.value: Cytogenetics

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A4
blocks.value: This is testing for abnormalities in the chromosomes. Certain genes are brought next to each other by a process called 'translocation' leading to uninterrupted growth and proliferation of white blood cell precursors in the bone marrow (e.g., Philadelphia Chromosome). Both are normal genes, but when they interact they produce an abnormal protein which drives the cancer. The absolute increase (Hyperdiploid) or decrease (Hypodiploid) in the number of chromosomes in the cancer cells are also features of ALL. Certain changes in sequence of the genes called mutations are also seen which have an impact on outcome of treatment.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_H5
blocks.value: Classification

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A5
blocks.value: Unlike cancers of solid organs, Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia and other blood cancers are not staged by the extent of involvement. Certain features related to the patient and the disease help in classifying the patients as: Standard-Risk or Good-Risk, Intermediate-Risk and High-Risk.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_LIST
value: Age: if the patient is 2–10 years old, it is considered favourable
Cytogenetics: Certain chromosomal abnormalities such as increase in number of chromosomes (Hyperploidy) and translocation are features of a favourable outcome.
Response to Treatment: Response to steroids after 7 days is probably a very good indicator of how the disease is going to respond to further treatment.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_H6
blocks.value: Minimal Residual Disease (MRD)

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A6
blocks.value: Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) means patient still has a minimum disease and has not fully recovered. Response to treatment is conventionally checked with a bone marrow morphology which establishes COMPLETE REMISSION (CR). However, CR means that the number of leukemia cells in the body are less than 10⁹ or 1000 million cells. At this level, they cannot be seen under the microscope.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ3_A7
blocks.value: MRD is the detection of cells below 1%, which can range from 1 in 1000 to 1 in a million cells. This enables us to check for response to treatment with a much higher sensitivity than possible under the microscope.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: Treatment Phases

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_LIST1
value: Induction Therapy: The first phase of treatment to kill most of the leukemia cells in the blood and bone marrow and restore normal blood cell production.
Consolidation Therapy: Also known as post-remission therapy. Aimed at destroying any remaining leukemia cells in the body.
Maintenance Therapy: Helps in preventing leukemia cells from regrowing. Treatments used in this stage are often given at lower doses over a long period of time.

Preventive treatment to the spinal cord: Patients suffering from acute lymphocytic leukemia may also receive treatment to kill leukemia cells located in the central nervous system. In this treatment, chemotherapy drugs are injected directly into the fluid that surrounds the spinal cord.

Radiation Therapy: Radiation therapy uses high-powered beams, such as X-rays, to kill cancer cells. If the cancer cells have spread to the central nervous system, your doctor may recommend radiation therapy.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_NOTE
blocks.value: Depending on patient's evaluation, the phases of treatment for acute lymphocytic leukemia can span up to 2-3 years.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_H2
blocks.value: Role of Chemotherapy

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_LIST2
value: 90% of the children with Standard Risk Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia are cured with Chemotherapy alone.
60-70% of children with Intermediate Risk Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia are cured with Chemotherapy alone.
40% of children with High Risk Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia are cured with Chemotherapy alone.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_A1
blocks.value: ALL has a tendency to spread to the brain. Hence, the treatment involves giving intrathecal chemotherapy periodically through a Lumbar Puncture. Intravenous chemotherapy is also given over 6 months, followed by oral drugs for 18-30 months. Boys are treated for a longer duration than girls.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_H3
blocks.value: Adolescents and Young Adults (AYA)

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ4_A2
blocks.value: Patients between ages 16 and 25 are called Adolescents and Young Adults (AYA). The results in this age group are slightly inferior than in younger children.

subsections.title: FAQ — Role of BMT in ALL

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_Q
blocks.value: Role of BMT in ALL

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_H1
blocks.value: When is BMT needed?

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_LIST1
value: Most children with Standard or Intermediate Risk Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia are cured with chemotherapy and do not need a BMT.
Most children with High Risk Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia might need a BMT.
Most Children with a positive MRD after intensive chemotherapy would benefit from a BMT.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_H2
blocks.value: Conditioning

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_A1
blocks.value: Total Body Radiation is an important component of conditioning for BMT.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_H3
blocks.value: Who can be a donor?

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_A2
blocks.value: Although we prefer a matched family donor, a Half matched (Haploidentical) family donor or an unrelated donor can also be used.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_H4
blocks.value: Results of BMT

blocks.label: ALL_PED_FAQ5_LIST2
value: BMT reduces the risk of relapse i.e. recurrence of disease by 50% compared to chemotherapy.
If the child is MRD negative before BMT, the risk of relapse is negligible.
BMT is the only cure for patients who relapse early after chemotherapy.

blocks.label: ALL_PED_OVERLINE
blocks.value: Pediatric BMT — Dr. Suparno Chakrabarti

blocks.value: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) in Children

subsections.title: FAQ — What is ALL? Causes & Symptoms (Adult)

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is ALL? Causes & Symptoms

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Acute Lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), is a cancer that starts from white blood cells called lymphocytes in the bone marrow (the soft inner part of the bones, where new blood cells are generated). The term "acute" means that the leukemia can progress quickly, and if not treated, would probably be fatal within a few months. 'Lymphoblastic' means it develops from early (immature) forms of lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia is the commonest childhood cancer. This arises from abnormalities in the precursors of normal lymphocytes which are otherwise a normal part of our immune system necessary to fight infections. These abnormal cells rapidly fill up the bone marrow space suppressing the normal cells. In most cases, the disease is characterised by certain abnormalities in the chromosomes which promote abnormal growth of certain lymphoid cells rather than maturation to normal lymphocytes.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: This is not known in most cases. However, exposure to radiation, exposure to high voltage electric wires and chemical exposures are few known risk factors.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ1_H1
blocks.value: Symptoms

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ1_LIST
blocks.hint: Symptoms listed as one paragraph on the site.
blocks.value: General weakness; Fatigue; High temperature (fever); Weight loss; Frequent infections; Bruising easily or with no obvious cause; Bleeding from the gums or nose; A fine rash of dark red spots (called purpura); Blood in urine or stools; Pain in the bones or joints; Breathlessness; Swollen lymph glands; Enlarged liver or spleen

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis, Classification & MRD

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count: The characteristic finding of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia is high white blood cell count with low haemoglobin and platelets.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_A2
blocks.value: Bone Marrow Examination: There must be more than 30% of abnormal cells (blasts) in the marrow.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_A3
blocks.value: Flow Cytometry: ALL has two broad subtypes- B cell and T cell ALL.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_A4
blocks.value: Cytogenetics: Testing for chromosomal abnormalities, including the Philadelphia Chromosome (translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22). The absolute increase (Hyperdiploid) or decrease (Hypodiploid) in the number of chromosomes are also features of ALL.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_A5
blocks.value: Classification: Standard-Risk or Good-Risk, Intermediate-Risk and High-Risk, based on age, cytogenetics, and response to treatment.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ2_A6
blocks.value: Minimal Residual Disease (MRD): Detection of cells below 1%, ranging from 1 in 1000 to 1 in a million cells — enables checking for response to treatment with much higher sensitivity.

subsections.title: FAQ — How is ALL in Adults Different? (Adult)

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: How is ALL in Adults Different? Treatment & BMT

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_H1
blocks.value: How is ALL in Adults Different from that in Children?

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_LIST1
value: ALL is less common in adults
Certain bad CYTOGENETICS such as Philadelphia Chromosome (translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22) are more common in adults.
Adults do not tolerate intensive chemotherapy as well as children
Only 30% of adults are cured by chemotherapy alone.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_H2
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: Treatment falls into phases: Induction, Consolidation, Maintenance, CNS prophylaxis, and Radiation if needed. Can span 2-3 years.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: ALL has a tendency to spread to the brain. Hence, the treatment involves giving intrathecal chemotherapy periodically through a Lumbar Puncture. Intravenous chemotherapy is also given over 6 months, followed by oral drugs for 18-30 months. Boys are treated for a longer duration than girls.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_H3
blocks.value: Bone Marrow Transplantation (BMT) for Adults with ALL

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_LIST2
value: Most adults with ALL benefit from a BMT at the first remission
Most adults beyond the age of 40 years cannot tolerate high doses of radiotherapy or chemotherapy and they are often treated with Reduced Intensity Conditioning BMT.
Once the disease recurs, the chance of achieving a second remission is much lower than in children.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_A3
blocks.value: Total Body Radiation is an important component of conditioning for BMT.

blocks.label: ALL_AD_FAQ3_A4
blocks.value: BMT reduces the risk of relapse by 50% compared to chemotherapy. BMT is the only cure for patients who relapse early after chemotherapy.

subsections.title: FAQ — What is Acute Myeloid Leukemia?

blocks.label: AML_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is Acute Myeloid Leukemia?

blocks.label: AML_FAQ1_A
blocks.value: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is a disorder of the process that normally produces neutrophils, a type of white blood cell. These abnormal cells rapidly fill up the bone marrow space, suppressing the normal cells. AML may sometimes be called Acute Myelogenous Leukemia, Acute Myelocytic Leukemia, and or Acute Nonlymphocytic Leukemia. Unlike chronic leukemia, acute leukemia develops quickly and generally needs immediate treatment. Acute Myeloid Leukemia is the most common type of acute leukemia which affects adults and chances of getting Acute Myeloid Leukemia increases with age.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Causes & Risk Factors

blocks.label: AML_FAQ2_INTRO
blocks.value: Acute Myeloid Leukemia is caused by damage to the DNA of developing cells in bone marrow. Though exact cause is not known, several risk factors have been identified:

blocks.label: AML_FAQ2_LIST
value: Treatment with chemotherapy or radiation therapy in the past
Treatment for childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in the past
Being exposed to radiation from an atomic bomb
History of a blood disorder such as myelodysplastic syndrome
Weakened immune system due to an organ transplant
Certain genetic disorders, including Down's syndrome and Fanconi's anaemia

blocks.label: AML_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Symptoms

blocks.label: AML_FAQ3_A
blocks.value: The symptoms are usually similar to ALL. In certain types of AML, like Acute Monoblastic Leukemia, or Acute Myelomonocytic Leukemia, one can have symptoms of gum swelling or skin nodules.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ3_LIST
blocks.value: Fever; Fatigue; Weight loss or loss of appetite; Shortness of breath; Anaemia; Easy bruising; Severe Bleeding; Petechiae (flat, pin-head sized spots under the skin caused by bleeding); Bone and joint pain; Persistent or frequent infections; Abnormal swelling of gums around teeth

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis & Classification

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A1
blocks.value: The characteristic finding of AML is high white blood cell count with low haemoglobin and platelet count.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H2
blocks.value: Peripheral Blood Smear

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A2
blocks.value: Presumptive diagnosis of AML can be made via examination of the peripheral blood smear when there are more than 20% blasts.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H3
blocks.value: Bone Marrow Examination

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A3
blocks.value: This is necessary for confirmation of the diagnosis. There must be more than 20% of abnormal cells (blasts) in the marrow for it to be called Acute Myeloid Leukemia. However, in certain cases of Acute Myeloid Leukemia, presence of the characteristic abnormality in chromosome is enough to call it Acute Myeloid Leukemia even with fewer than 20% blasts in the marrow.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H4
blocks.value: Flow Cytometry

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A4
blocks.value: AML has seven subtypes (AML-M1 ---- AML-M7). There are further subtypes in the individual categories which are confirmed via flow cytometry.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H5
blocks.value: Cytogenetics

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A5
blocks.value: This is testing for abnormalities in the chromosomes. Certain genes are brought next to each other by a process called 'translocation' leading to uninterrupted growth and proliferation of white blood cell precursors in the bone marrow. Missing pair of a chromosome in positions 5, 7, 8 is also a feature of AML. This is called Monosomy. Certain changes in sequence of the genes called mutations are also seen which have an impact on outcome of treatment.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H6
blocks.value: Classification

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A6
blocks.value: Unlike cancers of solid organs, ALL and other blood cancers are not staged by the extent of involvement of different organs. They are classified into: Standard-Risk or Good-Risk, Intermediate-Risk and High-Risk. This is predominantly based on the abnormality of chromosomes or genes.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_H7
blocks.value: Minimal Residual Disease (MRD)

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A7
blocks.value: Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) means patient still has a minimum disease and has not fully recovered. Response to treatment is conventionally checked with a bone marrow morphology which establishes COMPLETE REMISSION (CR). However, CR means that the number of leukemia cells in the body are less than 10⁹ or 1000 million cells. At this level, they cannot be seen under the microscope.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ4_A8
blocks.value: MRD is the detection of cells below 1%, which can range from 1 in 1000 to 1 in a million cells. This enables us to check for response to treatment with a much higher sensitivity than possible under the microscope.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_Q
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_H1
blocks.value: Chemotherapy

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_A1
blocks.value: Chemotherapy for AML is divided in two phases, INDUCTION and CONSOLIDATION. About 60-70% of patients achieve a complete remission. The chances of a sustained remission or cure are as follows:

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_H2
blocks.value: What is different about treatment of APML or AML-M3?

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_A2
blocks.value: This is a unique condition which responds to high doses of a form of Vitamin A called ALL-TRANS RETINOIC ACID (ATRA) and ARSENIC TRIOXIDE (ATO). This condition carries a 90% cure rate.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_H3
blocks.value: How is AML in Children Different from that in Adults?

blocks.label: AML_FAQ5_LIST2
value: AML is less common in children; particularly APML
Results of Chemotherapy are 10% better in children.
Children less commonly have BAD chromosomes.

subsections.title: FAQ — Role of BMT in AML

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_Q
blocks.value: Role of BMT in AML

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_H1
blocks.value: When is BMT needed for AML?

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_A1
blocks.value: Chemotherapy is curative in only one-third of patients with AML. All patients with AML apart from the ones with good risk genetics or APML should undergo BMT. BMT is best done when the disease is in first CR. Very few patients who RELAPSE or do not respond to treatment are cured.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_H2
blocks.value: Conditioning

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_A2
blocks.value: High to Moderate dose of chemotherapy is generally used in conditioning for BMT.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_H3
blocks.value: Who can be a donor?

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_A3
blocks.value: Although we prefer a matched family donor, a Half matched (Haploidentical) family donor or an unrelated donor can also be used. However, a HAPLOIDENTICAL DONOR who has Natural Killer Cell mismatch with the patient provides the best cure for AML via Graft versus Leukemia (GVL) effect.

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_H4
blocks.value: Results of BMT in AML

blocks.label: AML_FAQ6_LIST
value: BMT reduces the risk of relapse i.e., recurrence of disease by 80% compared to chemotherapy
If the patient is MRD negative before BMT, the risk of relapse is low
BMT is the only cure for patients, who relapse after chemotherapy

subsections.title: FAQ — What is CML in Children?

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is CML in Children? Natural History & Causes

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: A slowly progressing disease in which too many white blood cells (not lymphocytes) are made in the bone marrow. Chronic Myeloid Leukemia was the first disease where the molecular abnormality leading to cancer was deciphered. A unique translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 leads to the formation of BCR-ABL fusion protein which results in uncontrolled proliferation of white blood cells.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_H1
blocks.value: Phases of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_LIST1
value: Chronic phase: elevated blood counts with enlarged spleen. Patients are asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic.
Accelerated Phase: The disease starts getting out of hand. The spleen gets bigger despite treatment. The blasts in the blood and marrow start to increase (10 to 19%).
Blast Crisis: This is when the disease develops to a full blown leukaemia with marrow blasts more than 20%.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_H2
blocks.value: What Causes Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia?

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: This is largely unknown as in most cases of leukaemia. Risk factors: More common in males; More common in the elderly with a median age at diagnosis of 65 years; Exposure to ionising radiation; Low Immunity; Inflammatory bowel conditions, such as ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease; Using pesticides at work.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_H3
blocks.value: How is CML in Children different from that in Adults?

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ1_LIST2
value: CML is less common in children
Long term outcome of TKI therapy is not known in children. We are not talking of 10 or 20 years of treatment but for 50 to 60 years or more.
BMT should still be considered in children as first line therapy if the right donor is identified.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Symptoms, Diagnosis & Treatment

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: In most patients there are no symptoms, 25% of the patients are detected when the disease has progressed to accelerated or blast phase. Common symptoms include: Frequent Infections; Weight loss; Tiredness and looking pale; Swollen lymph glands; Abnormal bruising or bleeding; Abdominal discomfort due to an enlarged spleen; Poor appetite; Sweating at night; Headaches; Bone pain.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: Diagnosis

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ2_A2
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count: high white blood cell count with low haemoglobin and high platelet count. Bone Marrow Examination: necessary for confirmation; in chronic phase, blast count is less than 5%. Flow Cytometry: to confirm type of transformation. Cytogenetics: essential for confirmation of diagnosis. Karyotyping: establishes classic translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22. PCR: a test done on the DNA and establishes the amount of BCR/ABL product.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ2_H2
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ2_A3
blocks.value: Targeted therapy; Chemotherapy; Biologic therapy; High-dose chemotherapy with Bone Marrow Transplant.

subsections.title: FAQ — Role of BMT in CML

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Role of BMT in CML

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: BMT was the treatment of choice for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia until Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKI) were developed.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_H1
blocks.value: When is BMT needed?

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_LIST1
value: When patients stop responding to Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKI)
When Chronic Myeloid Leukemia is in Accelerated Phase or Blast Crisis.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_H2
blocks.value: Conditioning

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: High to Moderate dose of chemotherapy is generally used in conditioning for BMT in younger patients.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_H3
blocks.value: Who can be a donor?

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_A3
blocks.value: Although, a matched family donor is preferred, a Half matched (Haploidentical) family donor or an unrelated donor can also be used. However a Haploidentical (Half Matched) Donor who has Natural Killer Cell mismatch with the patient is an ideal donor for CML as it reduces the risk of relapse.

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_H4
blocks.value: Results of BMT in CML

blocks.label: CML_PED_FAQ3_A4
blocks.value: BMT remains the only curative treatment for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia.

blocks.label: CML_PED_OVERLINE
blocks.value: Pediatric BMT — Dr. Mahak Agarwal

blocks.label: CML_PED_TITLE
blocks.value: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) in Children

subsections.title: FAQ — What is CML in Adults?

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is CML in Adults? Natural History & Causes

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: A slowly progressing disease in which too many white blood cells (not lymphocytes) are made in the bone marrow which is also called Chronic Granulocytic Leukemia, Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia or Chronic Myeloid Leukemia. Chronic Myeloid Leukemia is more common in adults than in children. This arises from abnormality in Blood Stem Cells resulting in proliferation of both mature and immature white blood cells as well as platelets.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia was the first disease where the molecular abnormality leading to cancer was deciphered. A unique translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 results in formation of a protein which stimulates the enzyme Tyrosine Kinase leading to uncontrolled proliferation of white blood cells. This abnormality was called the PHILADELPHIA CHROMOSOME. The molecular basis for this was later established as the translocation of BCR gene to the ABL gene.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ1_H1
blocks.value: Phases of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ1_LIST
value: Chronic phase: elevated blood counts with enlarged spleen. Patients are asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic.
Accelerated Phase: The disease starts getting out of hand. The spleen gets bigger despite treatment. The blasts in the blood and marrow start to increase (10 to 19%).
Blast Crisis: This is when the disease develops to a full blown leukaemia with marrow blasts more than 20%.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: Risk factors: More common in males; More common in the elderly with a median age at diagnosis of 65 years; Exposure to ionising radiation; Low Immunity; Inflammatory bowel conditions; Using pesticides at work.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Symptoms, Diagnosis, Treatment & BMT

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: In most patients there are no symptoms, 25% of the patients are detected when the disease has progressed to accelerated or blast phase. Symptoms include: Frequent Infections; Weight loss; Tiredness; Swollen lymph glands; Abnormal bruising or bleeding; Abdominal discomfort; Poor appetite; Night sweats; Headaches; Bone pain.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_A2
blocks.value: Diagnosis: Complete Blood Count (high WBC, low Hb, high platelets); Bone Marrow Examination; Flow Cytometry; Cytogenetics; Karyotyping (establishes translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22); PCR (BCR/ABL level).

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_A3
blocks.value: Treatment: Targeted therapy; Chemotherapy; Biologic therapy; High-dose chemotherapy with BMT.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: BMT for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_A4
blocks.value: BMT was the treatment of choice for CML until TKI were developed. When is BMT needed: when patients stop responding to TKI; when CML is in Accelerated Phase or Blast Crisis.

blocks.label: CML_AD_FAQ2_A5
blocks.value: Conditioning: High to Moderate dose of chemotherapy in younger patients. A Haploidentical (Half Matched) Donor who has Natural Killer Cell mismatch with the patient is ideal. Results: Chronic Phase 90% cured; Accelerated Phase 40-60% cured; Blast Crisis 20-40% cured.

blocks.label: CML_AD_OVERLINE
blocks.value: Adult BMT — Dr. Suparno Chakrabarti

blocks.label: CML_AD_TITLE
blocks.value: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) in Adults

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is Hodgkin's Lymphoma? Types & Causes

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Hodgkin's lymphoma is a distinct cancer of lymphoid tissue derived from germinal B cells of lymph glands, defined by the presence of very characteristic cells called REED-STERNBERG cells or its variants with characteristic immunophenotype in a background of different types of cells.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_H1
blocks.value: Types of Hodgkin's Lymphoma

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_H2
blocks.value: What Causes Hodgkin's Lymphoma?

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: The exact cause of Hodgkin disease is not known. Most Hodgkin's lymphomas occur when an infection-fighting cell called a B cell develops a mutation in its DNA. The mutation tells the cells to divide rapidly and to continue living when a healthy cell would die.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_H3
blocks.value: Risk Factors

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ1_LIST2
value: Age: Generally Age group 15-35 and 55 years and above are diagnosed with Hodgkin's lymphoma.
Sex: Mostly Male are suspected to be on higher risk of Hodgkin's lymphoma.
Previously exposed to the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), a common virus that causes glandular fever.
Medical condition that weaken immune system, such as HIV.
Previous treatment with chemotherapy or radiotherapy for Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Symptoms

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ2_LIST1
value: Painless swelling of the lymph nodes in neck, armpits, groin (swollen glands)
Fever and chills that come and go (38°C / 100.4 F)
Unexplained Itching all over the body

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: Other symptoms that may occur

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ2_LIST2
value: Coughing, chest pains, or breathing problems if there are swollen lymph nodes in the chest
Pain or feeling of fullness below the ribs due to swollen spleen or liver
Pain in lymph nodes after drinking alcohol

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis & Staging

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ3_H1
blocks.value: Lymph Node Biopsy

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ3_A1
blocks.value: Hodgkin's lymphoma is diagnosed from Biopsy of lymph gland or other involved tissues. Histopathology reveals five subtypes: Nodular Sclerosis and Mixed cellularity are more common. Detection of cell surface antigens by Immunohistochemistry (IHC) is confirmatory. Expression of CD30 and CD15 are seen in the RS cells, except in NLPHL, where CD20 is expressed.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ3_A2
blocks.value: Blood tests; Imaging tests (X-rays, CT scan, PET); Bone Marrow Biopsy; Additional Tests: Serum LDH, ECG, Echocardiogram and Lung Function Test before starting treatment.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ3_H2
blocks.value: Staging

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ3_LIST
value: Stage I: Hodgkin disease is found in only 1 lymph node area or lymphoid organ such as the thymus. The cancer has not spread to other areas of the body.
Stage II: Hodgkin disease is found in 2 or more lymph node areas on the same side of (above or below) the diaphragm.
Stage III: Hodgkin disease is found in lymph node areas on both sides of (above and below) the diaphragm. It may also have spread to an area or organ near the lymph node areas or to the spleen.
Stage IV: Hodgkin disease has spread widely through 1 or more organs outside of the lymph system.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Treatment & Role of BMT

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: Treatment Options

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_LIST1
value: High-dose chemotherapy and Bone Marrow transplant

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A1
blocks.value: Favourable, Limited Stage Disease: Treated with short course chemotherapy and involved field radiotherapy; >95% cure rate.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A2
blocks.value: Unfavourable, Limited Stage Disease: Similar treatment but more intensive; > 90% cure rate.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A3
blocks.value: Advanced Disease: ABVD/BEACOPP >60%-80% cure rates.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A4
blocks.value: However, use of radiotherapy is not favoured in small children unless absolutely necessary.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_H2
blocks.value: BMT for Hodgkin's Lymphoma

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A5
blocks.value: About 30% patients with advanced disease and 10% patients with Limited disease relapse. Once Hodgkin's lymphoma relapses, the treatment is well-defined:

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_LIST2
value: Autologous Peripheral Blood Stem Cell Collection
High Dose Chemotherapy and Autologous BMT

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A6
blocks.value: If the patient is PET negative before BMT, 80% of the patients are cured. If the patient is PET positive, the cure rate is only 20%.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A7
blocks.value: If a patient achieves a complete remission, even in advanced disease, there is no need to perform a BMT upfront.

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_H3
blocks.value: When is Allogeneic BMT needed?

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_LIST3
value: When the disease recurs after Autologous BMT
When PBSC cannot be mobilised for Autologous BMT

blocks.label: HL_PED_FAQ4_A8
blocks.value: There is a strong Graft versus Tumour effect against HL in the setting of Allogeneic BMT. In the setting of High Risk and Relapsed Hodgkin's lymphoma, best results are obtained with HAPLOIDENTICAL DONOR and REDUCED INTENSITY CONDITIONING. 70% patients are cured with this procedure.

blocks.value: Pediatric BMT — Dr. Mahak Agarwal

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is Hodgkin's Lymphoma? Types, Causes & Symptoms

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Hodgkin's lymphoma is a distinct cancer of lymphoid tissue derived from germinal B cells of lymph glands, defined by the presence of very characteristic cells called REED-STERNBERG cells or its variants with characteristic immunophenotype in a background of different types of cells.

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: Types: Nodular sclerosing HL; Mixed-cellularity subtype; Lymphocyte-rich or Lymphocytic predominance; Lymphocyte depleted; Nodular lymphocyte predominant Hodgkin's lymphoma (NLPHL).

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: The exact cause of Hodgkin disease is not known. Most Hodgkin's lymphomas occur when an infection-fighting cell called a B cell develops a mutation in its DNA. The mutation tells the cell to divide rapidly and to continue living when a healthy cell would die.

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ1_A4
blocks.value: Symptoms: Painless swelling of lymph nodes in neck, armpits, groin; Fatigue; Fever and chills (38°C/100.4F); Unexplained Itching; Loss of appetite; Soaking night sweats; Unexplained Weight loss; Coughing/chest pains/breathing problems; Excessive sweating; Pain or fullness below ribs; Pain in lymph nodes after drinking alcohol; Skin blushing or flushing.

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis, Staging, Treatment & BMT

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_A1
blocks.value: Diagnosis by lymph node biopsy; Histopathology reveals subtypes; IHC is confirmatory (CD30 and CD15 in RS cells, CD20 in NLPHL); Blood tests; Imaging (X-rays, CT, PET); Bone Marrow Biopsy; Additional tests: Serum LDH, ECG, Echocardiogram, Lung Function Test.

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: Staging: I–IV

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_A2
blocks.value: Stage I: 1 lymph node area; Stage II: 2+ areas same side of diaphragm; Stage III: both sides; Stage IV: spread widely to organs outside lymph system.

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_H2
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_A3
blocks.value: Favourable Limited: >95% cure rate. Unfavourable Limited: >90% cure rate. Advanced: ABVD/BEACOPP 60–80%. BMT for relapsed cases: Salvage Chemo → Autologous PBSC collection → High Dose Chemo + Autologous BMT.

blocks.label: HL_AD_FAQ2_A4
blocks.value: PET negative before BMT: 80% cured. PET positive: 20% cure rate. Allogeneic BMT when disease recurs after Autologous BMT or PBSC cannot be mobilised. Haploidentical Donor with Reduced Intensity Conditioning: 70% cure rate.

blocks.value: Adult BMT — Dr. Suparno Chakrabarti

blocks.value: Hodgkin's Lymphoma in Adults

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ1_Q
blocks.value: What is Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma (NHL)?

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ1_A1
blocks.value: Lymphoma begins when B cells, T cells, or NK cells in the lymphatic system change and grow uncontrollably, which sometimes may form a tumor. Hodgkin lymphoma is a specific type of lymphoma that is covered in another section of this website. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) is a term that refers to the many other types of cancer of the lymphatic system, which can have different symptoms and signs, physical findings, and treatments.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ1_A2
blocks.value: Because lymphatic tissue is found in most parts of the body, NHL can start almost anywhere and can spread to almost any organ. It most often begins in the lymph nodes, liver, spleen, or bone marrow, but it can also involve the stomach, intestines, skin, thyroid gland, brain, or any other part of the body.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ1_A3
blocks.value: It is very important to know which type and subtype has been diagnosed because this information helps doctors determine the best treatment and a patient's prognosis (chance of recovery).

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ1_A4
blocks.value: Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma is more common in adults than children.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ2_Q
blocks.value: Causes & Risk Factors

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ2_INTRO
blocks.value: Certain types of Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma are caused or triggered by Infections. Most common causes are:

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ2_LIST1
value: Helicobacter Pylori causing lymphoma of the stomach.
HIV infection increases the incidence of high grade lymphomas
Long term intake of drugs suppressing the immune system as after solid organ transplants
Chemicals: polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), diphenylhydantoin, dioxin, and phenoxy herbicides.
Medical treatments: like radiation therapy and chemotherapy
Genetic diseases, like Klinefelter's syndrome, Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, ataxia telangiectasia syndrome
Autoimmune diseases, like Sjögren's syndrome, celiac sprue, rheumatoid arthritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ2_H1
blocks.value: Risk Factors

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ2_LIST2
value: Being male: Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma is more common in men than in women.
Age: Likelihood of getting Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma increases with age.
Impaired immune system: NHL is most common among those who have an impaired immune system, an autoimmune disease, or HIV or other viral infections.
Viral infections: A viral infection, such as Epstein-Barr virus, increases the risk of developing NHL.
Bacterial infections: Infection with Helicobacter pylori increases the risk of lymphoma involving the stomach.
Environmental exposure: Exposure to agricultural pesticides or fertilizers, solvents, and other chemicals may increase the risk of getting NHL.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ3_Q
blocks.value: Symptoms

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ3_INTRO
blocks.value: The symptoms are protean. They range from painless lymph node enlargement to fits and paralysis. Most commonly, patients present with 'B symptoms' defined by:

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ3_LIST
value: A painless swelling of the lymph nodes in the neck, underarms, or groin. This is the most common symptom of NHL.
Weight loss > 10% of baseline body weight
A cough or shortness of breath.
Pain in the belly or back.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_Q
blocks.value: Diagnosis

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_H1
blocks.value: Biopsy and Histopathology

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_A1
blocks.value: NHL is diagnosed from Biopsy of lymph gland or other involved tissues. Fine Needle aspiration Cytology (FNAC) is not enough for diagnosis of Lymphoma.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_A2
blocks.value: Histopathological diagnosis reveals two major subtypes: T cell Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma and B cell Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma. B cell Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma is more common. This is further classified as HIGH GRADE and LOW GRADE. Detection of cell surface antigens by Immunohistochemistry (IHC) is confirmatory.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_A3
blocks.value: Complete Blood Count: Anemia and Thrombocytopenia may be seen. Eosinophil count is often raised.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_A4
blocks.value: Bone Marrow Biopsy: This is necessary to ascertain the extent of the disease. Rarely, the presentation is with Bone Marrow and Blood involvement.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_A5
blocks.value: PET-CT Scan: This has revolutionised the diagnosis and management of NHL. In the early days, surgery was performed to stage the disease.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ4_A6
blocks.value: Additional Tests: Serum LDH, ECG, Echocardiogram and Lung Function Tests are done before starting treatment.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_Q
blocks.value: Treatment

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_A1
blocks.value: Treatment plans are designed to meet the unique needs of each person with lymphoma. Treatment decisions are made depending on: the type of NHL; the stage; how quickly the NHL is growing (grade); prognostic factors; person's age; person's overall health status; previous treatment, if any.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_H1
blocks.value: General Principles

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_A2
blocks.value: HIGH GRADE B CELL NHL: Chemotherapy called R-CHOP regimen remains the gold standard for primary treatment. However, based on GENE EXPRESSION PROFILE, Diffuse large B cell Lymphoma (DLBCL) is classified in three groups, GCB, ABC and PMBCL. The latter two groups have a poorer outcome and require upfront BMT. However, we do not favour use of radiotherapy in small children unless absolutely necessary.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_A3
blocks.value: LOW GRADE B CELL NHL: These lymphomas occur exclusively in older individuals and are treated in the same way as CLL.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_A4
blocks.value: T CELL NHL: They are mostly high grade and some of them are triggered by HTLV-1 virus. The response to CHOP is less encouraging. This group of patients require early BMT.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_H2
blocks.value: Treatment Options Other Than BMT

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ5_LIST
value: Chemotherapy: Single chemotherapy drugs or combinations of drugs can be given.
Biological therapy: May be used on its own or in combination with chemotherapy.
Radiation therapy: External beam radiation therapy may be used on its own to treat localized areas of early stage lymphoma.
Surgery: Surgery is mainly used to remove all or part of a lymph node (biopsy) to diagnose lymphoma.
Follow-up: It is important to have regular follow-up visits, especially during the first 2 years after treatment.

subsections.title: FAQ — Role of BMT in NHL

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_Q
blocks.value: Role of BMT in NHL

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_H1
blocks.value: What to do when Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma recurs?

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_A1
blocks.value: About 30% patients with advanced disease and 10% patients with Limited disease relapse. Once Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma relapses, the treatment is well-defined:

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_LIST1
value: Autologous Peripheral Blood Stem Cell Collection
High Dose Chemotherapy and Autologous BMT

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_H2
blocks.value: Cure Rate with Autologous BMT

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_A2
blocks.value: If the patient is PET negative before BMT, 80% of the patients are cured. If the patient is PET positive, the cure rate is only 20%.

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_H3
blocks.value: When is Upfront BMT Required?

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_H4
blocks.value: When is Allogeneic BMT Needed for Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma?

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_LIST3
value: When the disease recurs after Autologous BMT
When Peripheral Blood Stem Cells cannot be mobilised for Autologous BMT

blocks.label: NHL_FAQ6_A3
blocks.value: There is a strong Graft versus Tumour effect against NHL in the setting of Allogeneic BMT. In the setting of High Risk and Relapsed NHL, best results are obtained with HAPLOIDENTICAL DONOR and reduced toxicity transplantation. 70% patients are cured with this procedure. The children have more aggressive varieties of NHL. The cure rates in children are much higher.
